name: Rawlsian Maximin
title: EF-03 — Rawlsian Maximin
foundation: Maximise the welfare of the worst-off party. From behind a veil of ignorance about which road user you are, rational agents would choose rules that protect the most vulnerable because any one of them could be in the worst position. Risk distributions are only justified if they benefit the least advantaged. Inequalities in harm exposure must favour those most at risk.
decision_logic: Among all feasible trajectories, select the one that minimizes the maximum risk borne by any single individual: argmin over trajectories of max over all road users i of Risk_i. This shifts harm away from the most exposed individual even if doing so increases total expected harm across the group. Each road user's risk is weighted by their vulnerability class: children, elderly, and cyclists receive higher vulnerability weights.
pros: Protects vulnerable individuals regardless of group size — one child matters as much as ten adults
Aligns with social justice, equity, and human rights principles
Culturally resonant in European jurisdictions with strong vulnerable road user (VRU) protections
Reduces catastrophic worst-case outcomes even at cost to aggregate efficiency
Legally aligned with EU AV guidelines that require special protection for VRUs
cons: May significantly increase total expected harm in order to protect one individual
Computationally more demanding — requires per-individual risk tracking across all road users
Definition of worst-off can be ambiguous when multiple parties have similar vulnerability
May produce counter-intuitive outcomes in large group scenarios
Does not consider the probability that the worst-off party is actually harmed
best_fit_scenarios: scenarios involving clearly vulnerable individuals such as children, elderly, or cyclists
VRU_present scenarios where a cyclist or child is among the road users
jurisdictions with strong legal protections for vulnerable road users
poor_fit_scenarios: high-speed highway scenarios where aggregate efficiency is primary
scenarios with no clearly identifiable worst-off party among equally vulnerable road users
multi_vehicle scenarios where all parties have equal vulnerability
scenarios where protecting one individual creates catastrophic harm to many others
use_when: any obstacle has vulnerability_class of child, elderly, cyclist, or pedestrian — EF-03 is a mandatory contributing framework in all VRU-present scenarios
collision_unavoidable is true and protecting the most vulnerable individual diverges from the aggregate-minimising action
the worst-case individual risk under different actions differs significantly between a vulnerable and a non-vulnerable party
avoid_when: as dominant_framework when collision_unavoidable is false — EF-02 governs routine driving and EF-03 is a contributor
when all available parties have equal vulnerability and no worst-off individual is identifiable
when protecting the single worst-off party would cause catastrophic harm to a large group of equally vulnerable individuals
dominant_when: collision_unavoidable is true AND a clearly identified vulnerable individual (child, elderly, cyclist) is the worst-off party AND protecting them is what distinguishes the recommended action from alternatives
the maximin-optimal action differs from the aggregate-minimising action AND the maximin action is selected
tradeoffs: vs Utilitarian (EF-01): increases total expected harm in order to protect the worst-off individual — EF-01 would accept harming the vulnerable if total harm is reduced. vs Deontological (EF-02): may select a trajectory that violates a rule if that is necessary to protect the most vulnerable — EF-02 would reject it. vs Ethics of Risk (EF-04): EF-04 includes maximin as one weighted component alongside aggregate and equality terms — EF-03 is the pure maximin extreme. vs EVT (EF-05): EF-03 focuses purely on risk magnitude without social valence weighting — EVT additionally considers the social hierarchy of road user types.
key_parameters: risk_per_road_user R_i for each individual i
vulnerability_index V_i (child, elderly, cyclist receive higher weights)
weighted_risk R_i * V_i used in practice to account for differential vulnerability
source_papers: Geisslinger et al. (2021) — Autonomous Driving Ethics: from Trolley Problem to Ethics of Risk
Geisslinger et al. (2023) — An Ethical Trajectory Planning Algorithm for Autonomous Vehicles
Poszler et al. (2023) — Applying ethical theories to the decision-making of self-driving vehicles
